posts.hook: Your eyes have 6 muscles each. You train none of them. Here's what 2 minutes of eye rolling actually does.
posts.caption:
Six extraocular muscles move each eye in every direction.

You use them constantly — but you almost never take them through their full range of motion.

Screen work uses maybe 20% of that range.

Eye rolling (clock rotations) exercises:
→ Superior rectus (up)
→ Inferior rectus (down)
→ Medial rectus (inward)
→ Lateral rectus (outward)
→ Superior and inferior obliques (diagonal)

All 6 muscles. 2 minutes. Your eyes will feel looser than they have all day.

Guided exercise: axeo.vision/exercise

posts.hook: Eye rolling and clock rotations: the anatomy behind the exercise — and why your eye muscles need a full-range workout.
posts.caption:
Most people move their eyes in a tiny window all day.

Phone → keyboard → screen → keyboard. All within about 40° of arc.

Your eyes can move up to 45° in any direction.

The muscles that power this:
→ 4 rectus muscles (up, down, in, out)
→ 2 oblique muscles (diagonal, rotation)
→ All controlled by 3 cranial nerves (III, IV, VI)

When these muscles are underused:
- They tighten at their most-used angle
- Peripheral eye movements become less smooth
- Oculomotor fatigue accumulates unevenly

Clock rotations exercise:
1. Eyes only — keep head still
2. Slowly trace a clockwise circle — large as you can manage
3. Hold each 'hour' position for 1 second
4. 3 clockwise rounds, 3 counter-clockwise
5. Finish with tight spiraling circles inward, then outward

Your eyes should feel noticeably different afterward.

Guided exercise: axeo.vision/exercise

#EyeRolling #ClockRotations #EyeExercise #Axeo #AxeoVision #ExtraocularMuscles #EyeHealth

posts.hook: Eye rolling isn't an insult — it's an exercise. Here's the full extraocular muscle workout in 2 minutes.
posts.caption:
Six muscles move each eye. You're probably only using two of them today.

The screen + keyboard + screen pattern uses a narrow horizontal range.

Clock rotations exercise all six:

The technique:
1. Head still — eyes only
2. Look as far up as possible — hold 1 second
3. Slowly sweep clockwise — full circle — holding each extreme position
4. Complete 3 full clockwise rotations
5. Repeat 3 counter-clockwise
6. Finish: tight spiral circles, small to large, then large to small

Sensations during:
→ Resistance at positions rarely used — normal
→ Slight strain at the oblique (diagonal) positions — normal
→ Twitching or shaking — normal at first

Expect the first set to feel challenging in unexpected directions. That's the underused muscles waking up.

Free guided version with timing: axeo.vision/exercise

posts.hook: The 6 muscles that move your eyes — and why none of them get a proper workout during screen time.
posts.caption:
Six extraocular muscles. Three cranial nerves. Coordinated by the brainstem.

This system is one of the most complex motor systems in the human body.

And we sit them in a keyboard-to-screen-to-keyboard rut all day.

The muscles: rectus medialis (inward), rectus lateralis (outward), rectus superior (up), rectus inferior (down), obliquus superior (down-in rotation), obliquus inferior (up-out rotation).

Screen work exercises roughly: lateral, medial, and a narrow vertical band.

The obliques — which handle all diagonal and rotational movement — are almost never challenged by modern work patterns.

Result: uneven muscular development, asymmetric tension, and oculomotor fatigue that concentrates in specific vectors.

Clock rotations fix this. 2 minutes. All 6 muscles.

Guided version: axeo.vision/exercise

#ExtraocularMuscles #EyeExercise #ClockRotations #Axeo #EyeHealth #VisionTraining #EyeAnatomy

posts.hook: Your eye muscles get a workout during screen time — but only in about 30° of their total 270° range. Here's why that matters and what to do about it:
posts.caption:
1/ Six extraocular muscles control each eye: four rectus muscles (up, down, in, out) and two oblique muscles (diagonal and rotational movement). This system is controlled by cranial nerves III, IV, and VI — coordinated at the level of the brainstem.

2/ In normal modern screen-centric work, eye movement is predominantly horizontal and within a very narrow vertical band. The full arc of motion — especially the oblique vectors and the extremes of each rectus — is rarely engaged.

3/ The oblique muscles (superior oblique: CN IV, inferior oblique: CN III) handle intorsion and extorsion — rotational and diagonal eye movement. These are the muscles used for reading text on angled surfaces, tracking moving objects diagonally, and precise depth-shift work. Almost never needed at a desk.

4/ Underuse consequences: asymmetric oculomotor tension (fatigue concentrated in frequently used vectors), reduced smooth pursuit range, and occasionally vertical phoria — a small vertical misalignment between the two eyes that can cause subtle diplopia under fatigue.

5/ Clock rotations are a direct intervention: slowly tracing a full-circle path with the eyes (keeping head still) forces all six muscles through their full working range. 3 clockwise + 3 counter-clockwise rotations, holding at each extreme — total 2 minutes.

6/ Axeo has a guided clock rotation exercise at axeo.vision/exercise. Free, browser-based, includes technique reminders.

Friday is the best day to do this — end of a full week of narrow-range eye use.
posts.visual_notes: Educational thread. Anatomy-level detail but accessible. The oblique muscle insight is genuinely novel for most readers.

posts.hook: Eye rolling as exercise: the extraocular muscle workout I've been doing all week — and what I've noticed.
posts.caption:
r/EyeCare or r/HealthyHabits

Week 4, Day 5 of my eye exercise experiment. Today was clock rotations — also called eye rolling — which exercises all six extraocular muscles.

Had no idea the oblique muscles (the ones handling diagonal and rotational movement) are basically never challenged during desk work. I tried going to the 'lower right' extreme during the rotation and could genuinely feel resistance — like a muscle that hadn't been stretched in a while.

After 6 rounds (3 clockwise, 3 counter-clockwise):
- Eyes felt noticeably more mobile — hard to describe, but less 'locked' than usual
- The tightness behind my right eye that I'd chalked up to screen fatigue was reduced
- Distance objects seemed slightly cleaner for about 10 minutes afterward (probably accommodation releasing?)

For anyone curious about the anatomy: four rectus muscles handle the cardinal directions, two oblique muscles handle rotation and diagonal movement. Screen work essentially only uses the horizontal portion of the rectus range. Clock rotations hit everything.

Using axeo.vision/exercise for the guided version — it has a full protocol with timing. Free.

Week's been: 20-20-20 → Palming → Focus shifting → Brock String → Eye rotations. Tomorrow: figure-8 tracking. Then a full recap Sunday.

Anyone else done eye rotation exercises consistently for a few weeks? Curious about longer-term results.
